points.title: Sub-atomic particles
points.content: Proton: relative mass 1, charge +1, in nucleus. Neutron: relative mass 1, charge 0, in nucleus. Electron: relative mass 1/1840, charge ‚ąí1, outside nucleus.

points.title: Mass spectrometer steps
points.content: 1. Vaporisation ‚Üí 2. Ionisation (electron bombardment) ‚Üí 3. Acceleration ‚Üí 4. Deflection (magnetic field) ‚Üí 5. Detection. Ions with same m/z arrive together.

points.title: Calculating Ar from mass spectrum
points.content: Ar = ő£(isotopic mass √ó relative abundance) / ő£(relative abundances). Multiply each peak's m/z by its height/%, sum all, divide by total %.

points.title: Electron sub-shells order
points.content: Fill in order of increasing energy: 1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, 4p, 5s, 4d... Note: 4s fills before 3d but empties before 3d in transition metals.

points.title: Hund's rule
points.content: Electrons occupy orbitals of the same energy (degenerate orbitals) singly before pairing. All single electrons have parallel spins.

points.title: Ionisation energy trends
points.content: Across a period: generally increases (increasing nuclear charge). Down a group: decreases (increased shielding, greater distance from nucleus). Exceptions: slight drop at Group 3 (2p vs 2s) and Group 6 (pairing in 2p).

points.title: Emission spectrum of hydrogen
points.content: Electrons fall from higher to lower energy levels, releasing photons. Lyman series (UV): n‚Üí1. Balmer series (visible): n‚Üí2. Paschen series (IR): n‚Üí3. őĒE = hf = hc/őĽ.

points.title: Evidence for shells from IE
points.content: Large jumps in successive ionisation energies indicate change of shell. The jump position shows how many electrons are in the outer shell.

points.title: Shapes of orbitals
points.content: s orbital: spherical. p orbital: two lobes (dumbbell), three p orbitals (px, py, pz) perpendicular to each other. d orbitals: five d orbitals with more complex shapes.

points.title: Shielding effect
points.content: Inner electrons repel outer electrons, reducing the effective nuclear charge felt by outer electrons. More inner shells = more shielding = lower IE.

points.title: Mole calculations
points.content: n = m/M (mass/molar mass). n = V/24 (gas volume in dm¬≥ at RTP). n = cV (concentration √ó volume in dm¬≥). c = n/V.

points.title: Empirical vs molecular formula
points.content: Empirical = simplest ratio. Molecular = actual number of atoms. Molecular formula = n √ó empirical formula, where n = Mr(molecular) / Mr(empirical).

points.title: % composition by mass
points.content: % element = (n √ó Ar of element / Mr of compound) √ó 100%. Conversely: divide % by Ar, find simplest ratio ‚Üí empirical formula.

points.title: Ideal gas equation
points.content: pV = nRT. Use SI units: p in Pa, V in m¬≥, T in K, R = 8.314 J mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. At RTP (25¬įC, 100 kPa): Vm = 24.0 dm¬≥ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. At STP (0¬įC, 100 kPa): Vm = 22.7 dm¬≥ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ.

points.title: Limiting reagent method
points.content: 1. Calculate moles of each reactant. 2. Divide by stoichiometric coefficient. 3. Smaller value = limiting reagent. 4. Calculate product from limiting reagent moles.

points.title: Percentage yield
points.content: % yield = (actual yield / theoretical yield) √ó 100%. Theoretical yield calculated from limiting reagent via stoichiometry.

points.title: Atom economy
points.content: Atom economy = (Mr of desired product(s) / total Mr of all products) √ó 100%. Higher atom economy = greener process (less waste).

points.title: Concentration calculations
points.content: Molarity c (mol dm‚ĀĽ¬≥) = n/V. Dilution: c‚āĀV‚āĀ = c‚āāV‚āā. Titration: at equivalence point, moles acid from one source are stoichiometrically equivalent to base.

points.title: Volumetric analysis procedure
points.content: 1. Pipette known volume of one solution. 2. Add indicator. 3. Burette with other solution. 4. Titrate to endpoint. 5. Repeat for concordant results (within 0.1 cm¬≥). 6. Calculate moles, then unknown concentration.

points.title: VSEPR shapes
points.content: 2 bp: linear (180¬į). 3 bp: trigonal planar (120¬į). 4 bp: tetrahedral (109.5¬į). 3 bp + 1 lp: pyramidal (~107¬į). 2 bp + 2 lp: bent/V-shaped (~104.5¬į). 5 bp: trigonal bipyramidal. 6 bp: octahedral (90¬į).

points.title: Effect of lone pairs on bond angles
points.content: Lone pairs occupy more space than bonding pairs, reducing bond angles. Each lone pair reduces angles by ~2.5¬į. Order of repulsion: lp-lp > lp-bp > bp-bp.

points.title: Hybridisation summary
points.content: sp¬≥: tetrahedral (methane, water, ammonia). sp¬≤: trigonal planar, one unhybridised p orbital for ŌÄ bond (ethene, BF‚āÉ). sp: linear, two unhybridised p orbitals for two ŌÄ bonds (ethyne, CO‚āā, BeCl‚āā).

points.title: Electronegativity and bond polarity
points.content: If őĒEN > 1.7: ionic. If 0.4 < őĒEN < 1.7: polar covalent. If őĒEN < 0.4: non-polar covalent. Pauling values: F(4.0) > O(3.5) > N(3.0) > Cl(3.0) > Br(2.8) > C(2.5) > H(2.1).

points.title: Intermolecular forces comparison
points.content: Hydrogen bonds (~20 kJ/mol) > Permanent dipole-dipole (~2-5 kJ/mol) > Van der Waals/London (depends on size). VdW increases with Mr (more electrons = larger temporary dipoles).

points.title: Conditions for hydrogen bonding
points.content: Must have: H bonded directly to N, O, or F in one molecule AND a lone pair on N, O, or F in another molecule. Examples: H‚āāO (2 H-bonds per molecule), NH‚āÉ, HF, DNA base pairs.

points.title: Properties of ionic compounds
points.content: High melting/boiling points (strong ionic bonds). Conduct electricity when molten or dissolved (mobile ions). Hard but brittle. Soluble in polar solvents.

points.title: Giant covalent structures
points.content: Diamond: 4 C-C bonds, tetrahedral, very hard, no electrical conduction. Graphite: 3 C-C bonds, layers, delocalized ŌÄ electrons (conducts), lubricant. SiO‚āā: giant lattice, very hard, high mp.

points.title: Sigma and pi bonds
points.content: Single bond = 1 ŌÉ bond. Double bond = 1 ŌÉ + 1 ŌÄ bond. Triple bond = 1 ŌÉ + 2 ŌÄ bonds. Rotation is possible around ŌÉ bonds but NOT around ŌÄ bonds (restricted rotation in C=C causes geometric isomerism).

points.title: Metallic bonding and properties
points.content: Positive metal ions in a sea of delocalised electrons. High electrical/thermal conductivity (mobile electrons). Malleable/ductile (layers slide). High melting point (strong attraction). Increases with more delocalised electrons and smaller ion radius.

points.title: Kinetic theory assumptions
points.content: Molecules are point masses with negligible volume. No intermolecular forces. Collisions are perfectly elastic. Average KE ‚ąĚ absolute temperature (T in Kelvin). Molecules move in random directions.

points.title: Real vs ideal gas deviations
points.content: At high pressure: real gas volume > ideal (repulsive forces) or < ideal (attractive forces dominate). At low temperature: attractive forces significant ‚Üí real gas volume < ideal. Ideal behaviour: high T, low P.

points.title: Types of crystal structures
points.content: Ionic (NaCl): high mp, conducts when molten/dissolved. Giant covalent (SiO‚āā, diamond): very high mp, no conduction. Simple molecular (ice, I‚āā): low mp, no conduction. Metallic (Fe, Cu): high mp, conducts.

points.title: Allotropes of carbon
points.content: Diamond: sp¬≥, tetrahedral, hardest natural substance, insulator. Graphite: sp¬≤, layered, conductor (delocalised electrons), lubricant. Buckminsterfullerene (C‚āÜ‚āÄ): spherical, molecular, used in nanotechnology.

points.title: Effect of structure on melting point
points.content: Giant structures (ionic, covalent, metallic) have high melting points (must break many bonds). Simple molecular structures have low melting points (only overcome weak Van der Waals). Larger molecules have higher melting points (more VdW).

points.title: Standard conditions
points.content: Temperature: 298 K (25¬įC). Pressure: 100 kPa (1 bar). Concentration: 1 mol dm‚ĀĽ¬≥ for solutions. All substances in their standard states.

points.title: Hess's Law application
points.content: őĒHreaction = ő£őĒHf¬į(products) ‚ąí ő£őĒHf¬į(reactants). OR use a two-step Hess cycle: route 1 (direct) = route 2 (via intermediates). őĒHf¬į of elements in standard states = 0.

points.title: Bond enthalpy calculations
points.content: őĒH = ő£(bonds broken) ‚ąí ő£(bonds formed). Bonds broken: endothermic (+). Bonds formed: exothermic (‚ąí). Mean bond enthalpies give approximate values; exact values require specific compound data.

points.title: Calorimetry calculation
points.content: q = mcőĒT (m = mass of solution in g, c = 4.18 J g‚ĀĽ¬Ļ K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ for water, őĒT = temperature change). őĒH = ‚ąíq/n (negative sign: exothermic when T rises). Limitations: heat loss to surroundings, incomplete reaction.

points.title: Enthalpy profile diagrams
points.content: Exothermic: products lower than reactants, őĒH < 0. Endothermic: products higher than reactants, őĒH > 0. Activation energy (Ea) is always positive. Catalyst lowers Ea but does NOT change őĒH.

points.title: Definitions of standard enthalpies
points.content: őĒHf¬į: forming 1 mol compound from elements in standard states. őĒHc¬į: completely burning 1 mol substance. őĒHat¬į(element): forming 1 mol gaseous atoms from element. őĒHat¬į(compound): forming gaseous atoms from 1 mol compound.

points.title: Oxidation number rules
points.content: 1. Free element = 0. 2. Monatomic ion = ionic charge. 3. O = ‚ąí2 (except in peroxides ‚ąí1, OF‚āā +2). 4. H = +1 (except metal hydrides ‚ąí1). 5. Sum = overall charge of species.

points.title: Balancing half-equations
points.content: 1. Balance atoms other than O and H. 2. Balance O by adding H‚āāO. 3. Balance H by adding H‚Āļ. 4. Balance charge by adding e‚ĀĽ. 5. Combine half-equations (multiply to equalise electrons). Acidic conditions: use H‚Āļ/H‚āāO. Alkaline: use OH‚ĀĽ/H‚āāO.

points.title: Oxidising/reducing agent identification
points.content: The species that gains electrons (is reduced) is the oxidising agent. The species that loses electrons (is oxidised) is the reducing agent. OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain.

points.title: Common oxidising agents
points.content: MnO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ (KMnO‚āĄ): Mn +7 ‚Üí +2 (acidic) or +4. Cr‚āāO‚āá¬≤‚ĀĽ: Cr +6 ‚Üí +3. H‚āāO‚āā: O ‚ąí1 ‚Üí ‚ąí2. Cl‚āā: Cl 0 ‚Üí ‚ąí1. O‚āā: O 0 ‚Üí ‚ąí2. Fe¬≥‚Āļ: Fe +3 ‚Üí +2.

points.title: Common reducing agents
points.content: Fe¬≤‚Āļ ‚Üí Fe¬≥‚Āļ. SO‚āā/SO‚āÉ¬≤‚ĀĽ ‚Üí SO‚āĄ¬≤‚ĀĽ. I‚ĀĽ ‚Üí I‚āā. H‚āāO‚āā ‚Üí O‚āā (also oxidising agent). Metals (e.g. Fe, Zn) ‚Üí metal ions.

points.title: Disproportionation examples
points.content: Cl‚āā + H‚āāO ‚Üí HCl + HClO (Cl: 0 ‚Üí ‚ąí1 and +1). H‚āāO‚āā ‚Üí H‚āāO + O‚āā (O: ‚ąí1 ‚Üí ‚ąí2 and 0). Cu‚Āļ ‚Üí Cu + Cu¬≤‚Āļ.

points.title: Writing Kc expressions
points.content: For aA + bB ‚áĆ cC + dD: Kc = [C]^c √ó [D]^d / ([A]^a √ó [B]^b). Pure solids and liquids are NOT included (activity = 1). Units depend on őĒn = (c+d) ‚ąí (a+b).

points.title: Kp expressions and calculations
points.content: Kp = ő† p(products) / ő† p(reactants). Partial pressure p = mole fraction √ó total pressure. Mole fraction = n(gas) / n(total gas). Kp units: Pa raised to őĒn.

points.title: Effect of changes on equilibrium (Le Chatelier)
points.content: Increase concentration of reactant ‚Üí shift right (more products). Increase pressure (gas) ‚Üí shift to side with fewer moles of gas. Increase temperature ‚Üí shift in endothermic direction. Catalyst ‚Üí no effect on position, only reaches equilibrium faster.

points.title: Kc and temperature
points.content: Increasing T shifts equilibrium endothermic direction. If forward reaction is exothermic: Kc decreases with increasing T. If forward reaction is endothermic: Kc increases with increasing T. Catalyst does NOT affect Kc.

points.title: Haber process optimisation
points.content: N‚āā + 3H‚āā ‚áĆ 2NH‚āÉ, őĒH = ‚ąí92 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. High pressure ‚Üí higher yield (fewer moles gas on right). Low temperature ‚Üí higher yield (exothermic). But low T is slow ‚Üí compromise: 450¬įC, 200 atm, Fe catalyst.

points.title: Industrial equilibria trade-offs
points.content: Compromise conditions balance yield and rate. Very high P is expensive (engineering costs). Catalyst increases rate without affecting yield. Economic viability requires continuous process with recycling of unreacted gases.

points.title: Factors affecting rate
points.content: Temperature (‚ÜĎT ‚Üí ‚ÜĎrate; more particles exceed Ea, more frequent collisions). Concentration (‚ÜĎc ‚Üí ‚ÜĎrate; more frequent collisions). Pressure (gases: ‚ÜĎP ‚Üí ‚ÜĎrate). Surface area (solids: ‚ÜĎSA ‚Üí ‚ÜĎrate). Catalyst (lower Ea ‚Üí ‚ÜĎrate).

points.title: Collision theory
points.content: Reaction occurs when: 1. Particles collide. 2. With sufficient energy (‚Č• Ea). 3. With correct orientation. Rate ‚ąĚ frequency of successful collisions.

points.title: Boltzmann distribution and temperature
points.content: At higher T: curve shifts right, peak moves right and down, area under curve is same (total particles unchanged). Area to right of Ea increases significantly ‚Üí much greater fraction of molecules have energy ‚Č• Ea.

points.title: Determining reaction order from data
points.content: Zero order: rate independent of [A], [A] vs t is linear. First order: rate ‚ąĚ [A], ln[A] vs t is linear, constant t¬Ĺ. Second order: rate ‚ąĚ [A]¬≤, 1/[A] vs t is linear, t¬Ĺ increases with decreasing [A].

points.title: Rate equations and units
points.content: rate = k[A]^m[B]^n. Overall order = m + n. Units of k: for zero order: mol dm‚ĀĽ¬≥ s‚ĀĽ¬Ļ; first order: s‚ĀĽ¬Ļ; second order: dm¬≥ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ s‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. Doubling [A]: if 1st order ‚Üí rate √ó 2; if 2nd order ‚Üí rate √ó 4.

points.title: Catalysis mechanisms
points.content: Heterogeneous: reactants adsorb onto catalyst surface (active sites), bonds weaken, reaction occurs, products desorb. Example: Fe in Haber process. Homogeneous: catalyst in same phase, forms intermediate. Example: Mn¬≤‚Āļ catalysing H‚āāO‚āā decomposition.

points.title: Half-life of first order reactions
points.content: t¬Ĺ = ln 2 / k = 0.693 / k. Constant half-life regardless of initial concentration. If t¬Ĺ doubles when [A] halves ‚Üí NOT first order (second order has increasing t¬Ĺ).

points.title: Period 3 structure and bonding
points.content: Na, Mg, Al: metallic lattices. Si: giant covalent. P‚āĄ, S‚āą, Cl‚āā, Ar: simple molecular. This explains the trend in melting points: increases Na‚ÜíSi, then drops sharply P‚ÜíAr.

points.title: Period 3 oxide reactions with water
points.content: Na‚āāO: strongly alkaline (Na‚āāO + H‚āāO ‚Üí 2NaOH). MgO: weakly alkaline. Al‚āāO‚āÉ: amphoteric (reacts with both acid and alkali). SiO‚āā: insoluble (giant covalent). P‚āĄO‚āĀ‚āÄ: weakly/strongly acidic. SO‚āā: weakly acidic. SO‚āÉ: strongly acidic. Cl‚āāO‚āá: very strongly acidic.

points.title: Period 3 chlorides with water
points.content: NaCl: neutral solution (ionic, dissociates). MgCl‚āā: slightly acidic. AlCl‚āÉ: acidic (Al¬≥‚Āļ hydrolyses). SiCl‚āĄ: fumes of HCl, forms SiO‚āā + HCl. PCl‚āÉ: H‚āÉPO‚āÉ + HCl. PCl‚āÖ: H‚āÉPO‚āĄ + HCl. SCl‚āā: very acidic.

points.title: Trends in atomic radius
points.content: Decreases across a period (increasing Zeff, same shell). Increases down a group (more electron shells, increased shielding). Transition metals: similar radii due to shielding of d electrons.

points.title: Trends in electronegativity
points.content: Increases across a period (increasing Zeff). Decreases down a group (increased shielding and atomic radius). Most electronegative: F (4.0). Pauling scale values used in A-level.

points.title: Ionisation energy anomalies in Period 2
points.content: Drop from Be to B: B has one 2p electron, easier to remove than 2s. Drop from N to O: O has a paired 2p electron (extra repulsion), easier to remove than N's unpaired electron.

points.title: Reactions of Group 2 metals
points.content: With O‚āā: 2M + O‚āā ‚Üí 2MO (Mg burns brilliantly white; Ba forms BaO‚āā). With water: M + 2H‚āāO ‚Üí M(OH)‚āā + H‚āā (Mg reacts slowly; Ca‚ÄďBa react vigorously). With dilute acids: M + H‚āāSO‚āĄ ‚Üí MSO‚āĄ + H‚āā.

points.title: Solubility trends
points.content: Hydroxides: Mg(OH)‚āā sparingly soluble ‚Üí Ba(OH)‚āā soluble (increases down group). Sulfates: MgSO‚āĄ soluble ‚Üí BaSO‚āĄ insoluble (decreases down group). CaSO‚āĄ sparingly soluble (scale in pipes).

points.title: Thermal stability of carbonates
points.content: MCO‚āÉ ‚Üí MO + CO‚āā. Stability increases down group (Mg easier to decompose; BaCO‚āÉ requires higher T). Small, highly charged cation (Mg¬≤‚Āļ) polarises CO‚āÉ¬≤‚ĀĽ more ‚Üí destabilises ‚Üí decomposes at lower T.

points.title: Uses of Group 2 compounds
points.content: Ca(OH)‚āā (slaked lime): neutralises acidic soils, flue gas desulfurisation. CaCO‚āÉ (limestone): building material, neutralisation. BaSO‚āĄ: barium meal (X-ray contrast agent, insoluble so non-toxic). MgO: refractory (high mp).

points.title: Flame colours
points.content: Mg: no characteristic colour (bright white light). Ca: brick-red. Sr: crimson. Ba: green.

points.title: Physical properties trends
points.content: Boiling point: F‚āā < Cl‚āā < Br‚āā < I‚āā (increases with Mr, stronger VdW forces). State at RTP: F‚āā and Cl‚āā (gases), Br‚āā (liquid), I‚āā (solid). Colour: F‚āā (pale yellow), Cl‚āā (green-yellow), Br‚āā (red-brown), I‚āā (dark grey/purple vapour).

points.title: Halogen displacement reactions
points.content: A more reactive halogen displaces a less reactive one from its salt. Cl‚āā + 2Br‚ĀĽ ‚Üí Br‚āā + 2Cl‚ĀĽ. Cl‚āā + 2I‚ĀĽ ‚Üí I‚āā + 2Cl‚ĀĽ. Br‚āā + 2I‚ĀĽ ‚Üí I‚āā + 2Br‚ĀĽ. F‚āā displaces all; I‚āā displaces none. Use organic solvent to identify halogen by colour.

points.title: Reactions with NaOH
points.content: Cold dilute NaOH: Cl‚āā + 2NaOH ‚Üí NaCl + NaClO + H‚āāO (bleach/disinfectant). Hot concentrated NaOH: 3Cl‚āā + 6NaOH ‚Üí 5NaCl + NaClO‚āÉ + 3H‚āāO. Both are disproportionation reactions.

points.title: Testing for halide ions
points.content: Add dilute HNO‚āÉ (to remove CO‚āÉ¬≤‚ĀĽ, SO‚āÉ¬≤‚ĀĽ interferences), then AgNO‚āÉ(aq). AgCl: white precipitate, soluble in dilute NH‚āÉ. AgBr: cream precipitate, soluble in concentrated NH‚āÉ. AgI: yellow precipitate, insoluble in NH‚āÉ.

points.title: Hydrogen halide bond strengths
points.content: HF > HCl > HBr > HI (bond enthalpy decreases, acid strength increases). HF is a weak acid (strong H-bond). HCl, HBr, HI are strong acids. HI is the strongest reducing agent (I‚ĀĽ can reduce H‚āāSO‚āĄ to H‚āāS).

points.title: Reactions of halide ions with H‚āāSO‚āĄ
points.content: NaCl + H‚āāSO‚āĄ ‚Üí NaHSO‚āĄ + HCl (only acid-base, Cl‚ĀĽ not strong enough to reduce H‚āāSO‚āĄ). NaBr + H‚āāSO‚āĄ ‚Üí fumes of HBr, also some Br‚āā and SO‚āā. NaI + H‚āāSO‚āĄ ‚Üí HI, I‚āā, H‚āāS, S (more vigorous reduction due to stronger reducing power of I‚ĀĽ).

points.title: Haber Process
points.content: N‚āā + 3H‚āā ‚áĆ 2NH‚āÉ, őĒH = ‚ąí92 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. Conditions: 450¬įC, 200 atm, iron catalyst with K‚āāO/Al‚āāO‚āÉ promoter. ~15% yield per pass; unreacted gases recycled. Trade-off: lower T ‚Üí higher yield but slower rate.

points.title: Ostwald Process (HNO‚āÉ manufacture)
points.content: 1. 4NH‚āÉ + 5O‚āā ‚Üí 4NO + 6H‚āāO (Pt/Rh catalyst, 900¬įC). 2. 2NO + O‚āā ‚Üí 2NO‚āā. 3. 4NO‚āā + O‚āā + 2H‚āāO ‚Üí 4HNO‚āÉ.

points.title: Contact Process (H‚āāSO‚āĄ manufacture)
points.content: 1. S + O‚āā ‚Üí SO‚āā. 2. 2SO‚āā + O‚āā ‚áĆ 2SO‚āÉ (V‚āāO‚āÖ catalyst, 450¬įC, 1 atm). 3. SO‚āÉ + H‚āāSO‚āĄ ‚Üí H‚āāS‚āāO‚āá (oleum). 4. H‚āāS‚āāO‚āá + H‚āāO ‚Üí 2H‚āāSO‚āĄ. Cannot add water directly to SO‚āÉ (too vigorous, acidic mist).

points.title: Environmental issues
points.content: SO‚āā: causes acid rain (2SO‚āā + O‚āā + 2H‚āāO ‚Üí 2H‚āāSO‚āĄ); removed by FGD using CaCO‚āÉ or Ca(OH)‚āā. NO‚āď: from vehicle engines, contributes to acid rain and photochemical smog. Catalytic converters: 2CO + 2NO ‚Üí 2CO‚āā + N‚āā (Pt/Rh).

points.title: Properties of ammonia
points.content: Weak base (pKb ~4.7). Reacts with acids: NH‚āÉ + HCl ‚Üí NH‚āĄCl. Ligand (lone pair on N): forms [Cu(NH‚āÉ)‚āĄ]¬≤‚Āļ (deep blue) with Cu¬≤‚Āļ after dissolving Cu(OH)‚āā precipitate. Test: moist red litmus turns blue.

points.title: Types of structural isomers
points.content: Chain isomers: different carbon skeletons. Position isomers: same functional group in different positions. Functional group isomers: same formula, different functional groups (e.g. alcohol vs ether, aldehyde vs ketone).

points.title: Geometric isomerism conditions
points.content: Requires: C=C double bond (or ring) AND two different groups on each carbon of the double bond. Cis: same groups on same side. Trans: same groups on opposite sides. Note: if either carbon has two identical groups, no geometric isomerism.

points.title: Optical isomerism
points.content: Requires a chiral carbon (4 different groups). Two enantiomers are non-superimposable mirror images. Racemic mixture (racemate): equal amounts of both enantiomers, optically inactive. Plane-polarised light: (+) enantiomer rotates clockwise; (‚ąí) anticlockwise.

points.title: Reaction mechanisms types
points.content: Free radical substitution (alkanes + Cl‚āā/Br‚āā, UV). Electrophilic addition (alkenes). Nucleophilic substitution (halogenoalkanes). Electrophilic substitution (benzene). Nucleophilic addition (carbonyls). Addition-elimination / condensation.

points.title: Drawing mechanisms
points.content: Use curly arrows to show electron pair movement. Arrow starts from electron source (bond or lone pair) ‚Üí goes to electron sink (electrophilic centre). Show all intermediates (carbocations, carbanions, radicals). For ionic mechanisms: show charges clearly.

points.title: Naming organic compounds (IUPAC)
points.content: 1. Find longest carbon chain = parent name. 2. Number from end nearest to substituents/functional groups. 3. Prefix substituents alphabetically with position numbers. 4. Suffix = principal functional group (ol, al, one, oic acid, amine, amide, ester). Ester: alkyl followed by parent acid -anoate.

points.title: Alkane reactions
points.content: Combustion: C‚āôH‚āā‚āô‚āä‚āā + O‚āā ‚Üí CO‚āā + H‚āāO (complete). Free radical substitution: UV light required. Chain: initiation (Cl‚āā ‚Üí 2Cl‚ÄĘ), propagation (Cl‚ÄĘ + CH‚āĄ ‚Üí CH‚āÉ‚ÄĘ + HCl; CH‚āÉ‚ÄĘ + Cl‚āā ‚Üí CH‚āÉCl + Cl‚ÄĘ), termination (radical + radical ‚Üí molecule).

points.title: Alkene reactions
points.content: Electrophilic addition: H‚āā (Ni catalyst, hydrogenation), HX (Markovnikov), H‚āāO (H‚āÉPO‚āĄ, steam, hydration), X‚āā (halogenation, test for C=C), KMnO‚āĄ/H‚Āļ (oxidation, test). Polymerisation. Mechanism: electrophile attacks ŌÄ bond ‚Üí carbocation ‚Üí nucleophile attacks.

points.title: Test for alkenes
points.content: Add bromine water (orange-brown): alkenes decolourise immediately (room temperature, no UV). Alkanes do NOT decolourise bromine water at room temperature. KMnO‚āĄ: alkenes decolourise purple ‚Üí colourless (or brown MnO‚āā in neutral conditions).

points.title: Markovnikov's rule explained
points.content: In addition of HX to unsymmetrical alkene, H adds to carbon with more H atoms. Reason: more substituted carbocation (2¬į or 3¬į) is more stable than 1¬į (alkyl groups donate electron density, stabilising + charge). Major product from more stable intermediate.

points.title: Cracking
points.content: Thermal cracking: high T (>800¬įC), high P, long chain ‚Üí shorter chains + alkenes (free radical mechanism). Catalytic cracking: zeolite catalyst, ~500¬įC, produces more branched alkanes (better octane rating for fuels). Steam cracking: produces ethene for plastics.

points.title: Petroleum fractionation
points.content: Crude oil separated by fractional distillation. Fractions by decreasing boiling point: bitumen > fuel oil > diesel > kerosene > petrol > refinery gas. More C atoms ‚Üí higher bp (stronger VdW). Reforming converts straight-chain to branched-chain alkanes.

points.title: SN1 vs SN2 mechanisms
points.content: SN2: one step, bimolecular, inversion of configuration (Walden inversion), favoured by primary halogenoalkanes and strong nucleophiles. SN1: two steps (carbocation intermediate), unimolecular, racemisation, favoured by tertiary halogenoalkanes and polar solvents.

points.title: Nucleophilic substitution reactions
points.content: With NaOH(aq): ‚Üí alcohol (OH‚ĀĽ nucleophile, warm). With KCN(alc): ‚Üí nitrile (CN‚ĀĽ nucleophile, chain extended by 1C). With NH‚āÉ(alc, excess, sealed tube): ‚Üí amine. With AgNO‚āÉ(aq): ‚Üí silver halide precipitate (tests for halide after hydrolysis).

points.title: Elimination reaction
points.content: Halogenoalkane + hot concentrated KOH in ethanol ‚Üí alkene + KX + H‚āāO. Favoured by: tertiary halogenoalkanes, hot conditions, concentrated base. Competes with substitution (aqueous NaOH favours substitution; alcoholic KOH favours elimination).

points.title: Rate of hydrolysis
points.content: Add aqueous AgNO‚āÉ + ethanol (warm). Time for precipitate: C‚ÄďI fastest (weakest bond), C‚ÄďBr medium, C‚ÄďCl slowest. C‚ÄďF does not hydrolyse (too strong). This shows bond strength, NOT electronegativity, determines rate.

points.title: Environmental impact of CFCs
points.content: CFCs (e.g. CCl‚āāF‚āā) are very stable. In stratosphere: UV ‚Üí Cl‚ÄĘ radicals. Chain reaction: Cl‚ÄĘ + O‚āÉ ‚Üí ClO‚ÄĘ + O‚āā; ClO‚ÄĘ + O‚ÄĘ ‚Üí Cl‚ÄĘ + O‚āā. One Cl‚ÄĘ destroys thousands of O‚āÉ molecules. Replacement: HCFCs (less persistent) and HFCs (no Cl atoms).

points.title: Reactions of alcohols
points.content: Combustion: complete burning. Oxidation: 1¬į ‚Üí aldehyde (distil off) ‚Üí carboxylic acid. 2¬į ‚Üí ketone. 3¬į ‚Üí no oxidation. Reagent: K‚āāCr‚āāO‚āá/H‚āāSO‚āĄ (orange ‚Üí green). Dehydration: Al‚āāO‚āÉ catalyst (180¬įC) ‚Üí alkene. Esterification: carboxylic acid + H‚āāSO‚āĄ catalyst (reversible). Reaction with Na: releases H‚āā.

points.title: Esterification mechanism and conditions
points.content: RCOOH + R'OH ‚áĆ RCOOR' + H‚āāO. Acid catalyst (H‚āāSO‚āĄ or H‚āÉPO‚āĄ), warm. Equilibrium: excess alcohol or acid, remove water to improve yield. Reverse (hydrolysis): add water + acid catalyst (or NaOH for irreversible saponification).

points.title: Properties of carboxylic acids
points.content: Weak acids (partially dissociate). Stronger than carbonic acid ‚Üí react with carbonates (CO‚āā produced). H-bonding (dimer formation) ‚Üí relatively high bp. React with: Na, NaOH, Na‚āāCO‚āÉ/NaHCO‚āÉ (all give CO‚āā with carbonates).

points.title: Distinguishing aldehydes from carboxylic acids
points.content: Both contain C=O. Carboxylic acid: reacts with Na‚āāCO‚āÉ (effervescence). Aldehyde: does NOT react with Na‚āāCO‚āÉ; gives silver mirror with Tollens'; gives brick-red precipitate with Fehling's solution.

points.title: Biodiesel and fats
points.content: Vegetable oils/animal fats: triglycerides (esters of glycerol + 3 fatty acids). Transesterification with methanol (NaOH catalyst): ‚Üí biodiesel (FAME: fatty acid methyl esters) + glycerol. Biodiesel: renewable, lower net CO‚āā emissions.

points.title: Nucleophilic addition mechanism to carbonyl
points.content: Step 1: nucleophile (e.g. CN‚ĀĽ, HCN) attacks the őī+ carbon of C=O. Step 2: protonation of O‚ĀĽ intermediate by H‚Āļ (or H‚āāO). Product: addition compound (alcohol). Example: RCHO + HCN ‚Üí RCH(OH)CN (hydroxynitrile).

points.title: Reduction of carbonyls
points.content: Reagent: NaBH‚āĄ (in water/ethanol) or LiAlH‚āĄ (in dry ether). Aldehyde ‚Üí primary alcohol. Ketone ‚Üí secondary alcohol. Mechanism: H‚ĀĽ (hydride ion) is the nucleophile attacking C=O carbon. NaBH‚āĄ is safer (less vigorous) than LiAlH‚āĄ.

points.title: Tests to distinguish aldehydes and ketones
points.content: Tollens' reagent [Ag(NH‚āÉ)‚āā]‚Āļ: aldehyde ‚Üí silver mirror (Ag deposited); ketone ‚Üí no reaction. Fehling's solution (Cu¬≤‚Āļ in NaOH/tartrate): aldehyde ‚Üí brick-red Cu‚āāO precipitate; ketone ‚Üí no reaction. Both oxidise aldehyde (RCHO ‚Üí RCOOH).

points.title: Iodoform reaction (triiodomethane test)
points.content: Reagent: I‚āā/NaOH. Positive result (pale yellow CHI‚āÉ precipitate + antiseptic smell) with: methyl ketones (CH‚āÉCOR), ethanol (CH‚āÉCH‚āāOH), ethanal (CH‚āÉCHO). Tests for CH‚āÉC=O or CH‚āÉCHOH group.

points.title: Aldol condensation
points.content: Two carbonyl compounds (with őĪ-H) react under dilute NaOH (cold) ‚Üí ő≤-hydroxy carbonyl (aldol). On heating: dehydration ‚Üí őĪ,ő≤-unsaturated carbonyl. Important in forming C‚ÄďC bonds.

points.title: Cyanohydrin formation and use
points.content: RCH=O + HCN ‚Üí RCH(OH)CN (with KCN/H‚Āļ or NaCN). Creates new chiral centre ‚Üí racemic mixture. Cyanohydrin can be hydrolysed to őĪ-hydroxy acid or reduced to amino alcohol. Extends carbon chain by 1.

points.title: Born-Haber cycle construction
points.content: Steps: 1. őĒHf¬į (formation of ionic compound). 2. őĒHat¬į of metal. 3. őĒHat¬į of non-metal. 4. IE (ionisation energy of metal). 5. EA (electron affinity of non-metal). 6. Lattice energy (őĒHlat). By Hess's Law: őĒHf¬į = őĒHat¬į(M) + őĒHat¬į(X) + IE + EA + őĒHlat.

points.title: Factors affecting lattice energy
points.content: Magnitude of lattice energy increases with: smaller ionic radius (ions closer together ‚Üí stronger attraction) and higher ionic charge (more Coulombic force). |őĒHlat| order: MgO > CaO > NaCl > KCl. Na‚Āļ < K‚Āļ radius ‚Üí NaCl more exothermic.

points.title: Theoretical vs experimental lattice energy
points.content: For NaCl: theoretical (purely ionic) ‚Čą experimental. For AgCl: experimental őĒHlat more negative than theoretical ‚Üí greater covalent character (Ag‚Āļ is polarising, Cl‚ĀĽ is polarisable). Discrepancy indicates partial covalent character.

points.title: Enthalpy of solution cycle
points.content: őĒHsol = ‚ąíőĒHlat(lattice dissociation) + őĒHhyd(cation) + őĒHhyd(anion). If őĒHhyd overcomes őĒHlat ‚Üí soluble (őĒHsol negative or small positive). NaCl: slightly endothermic (+3.9 kJ/mol) but entropy drives dissolution.

points.title: Hydration enthalpy trends
points.content: More exothermic (more negative) for: smaller ionic radius (ion-dipole interactions stronger), higher charge. Li‚Āļ > Na‚Āļ > K‚Āļ. Mg¬≤‚Āļ >> Na‚Āļ. Anions: F‚ĀĽ >> I‚ĀĽ (smaller, higher charge density).

points.title: Standard electrode potential measurement
points.content: Requires: standard hydrogen electrode (SHE, E¬į = 0.00 V) as reference. Standard conditions: 298 K, 1 mol dm‚ĀĽ¬≥ ion concentration, 100 kPa for gases. Connect with salt bridge (KNO‚āÉ) to complete circuit.

points.title: Predicting cell EMF and spontaneity
points.content: E¬įcell = E¬į(cathode) ‚ąí E¬į(anode) = E¬į(more +ve) ‚ąí E¬į(less +ve). If E¬įcell > 0: reaction is spontaneous (feasible). The species with more positive E¬į is reduced (is the oxidising agent). Reaction at anode: oxidation. Cathode: reduction.

points.title: Limitations of E¬į predictions
points.content: E¬į values predict thermodynamic feasibility only. Kinetic barriers (high Ea) may prevent reaction. Conditions (non-standard T, concentration) affect actual potentials (Nernst equation). A reaction may be feasible but imperceptibly slow.

points.title: Electrolysis: predicting products
points.content: At cathode (‚ąí): cation with most positive (least negative) E¬į is discharged. At anode (+): anion with most negative E¬į is discharged. In practice: overpotential means Cl‚ĀĽ discharged preferentially over OH‚ĀĽ in concentrated NaCl(aq). At inert anode: O‚āā from dilute solutions, Cl‚āā from concentrated Cl‚ĀĽ.

points.title: Faraday's laws calculations
points.content: Q = It (charge = current √ó time). Moles of electrons = Q/F (F = 96485 C mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ ‚Čą 96500). Moles of product = moles electrons / number of electrons in half-equation. Mass = moles √ó molar mass.

points.title: Fuel cells
points.content: H‚āā-O‚āā fuel cell: H‚āā + ¬ĹO‚āā ‚Üí H‚āāO. Anode: H‚āā ‚Üí 2H‚Āļ + 2e‚ĀĽ. Cathode: ¬ĹO‚āā + 2H‚Āļ + 2e‚ĀĽ ‚Üí H‚āāO. Advantages: higher efficiency than combustion engine, only water produced. Disadvantages: H‚āā storage, expensive catalysts (Pt).

points.title: pH calculations
points.content: Strong acid: [H‚Āļ] = c(acid) ‚Üí pH = ‚ąílog(c). Strong base: [OH‚ĀĽ] = c(base), pOH = ‚ąílog[OH‚ĀĽ], pH = 14 ‚ąí pOH. Weak acid: [H‚Āļ] = ‚ąö(Ka √ó c) assuming Ka << c. Buffer: [H‚Āļ] = Ka √ó [HA]/[A‚ĀĽ], pH = pKa + log([A‚ĀĽ]/[HA]).

points.title: Buffer action explained
points.content: Buffer = weak acid HA + its salt (A‚ĀĽ). Add H‚Āļ: HA ‚áĆ H‚Āļ + A‚ĀĽ shifts left (H‚Āļ + A‚ĀĽ ‚Üí HA). Add OH‚ĀĽ: HA + OH‚ĀĽ ‚Üí A‚ĀĽ + H‚āāO (weak acid neutralises OH‚ĀĽ). pH ‚Čą pKa when [HA] = [A‚ĀĽ] (half-equivalence point).

points.title: Indicators and titration curves
points.content: Indicator works at pH = pKIn ¬Ī 1. Choose indicator whose pKIn falls in the vertical part of the titration curve. Strong acid‚Äďstrong base: any indicator (phenolphthalein or methyl orange). Weak acid‚Äďstrong base: phenolphthalein. Strong acid‚Äďweak base: methyl orange. Weak acid‚Äďweak base: no sharp endpoint.

points.title: Hydrolysis of salts
points.content: NH‚āĄCl: NH‚āĄ‚Āļ + H‚āāO ‚áĆ NH‚āÉ + H‚āÉO‚Āļ ‚Üí acidic. Na‚āāCO‚āÉ: CO‚āÉ¬≤‚ĀĽ + H‚āāO ‚áĆ HCO‚āÉ‚ĀĽ + OH‚ĀĽ ‚Üí alkaline. NaCl: no hydrolysis ‚Üí neutral. Al(NO‚āÉ)‚āÉ: Al¬≥‚Āļ hydrolyses ‚Üí acidic.

points.title: Kw and its temperature dependence
points.content: Kw = [H‚Āļ][OH‚ĀĽ] = 10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ‚Āī at 298 K. pH of pure water = 7 at 298 K. As T increases, Kw increases (ionisation of water is endothermic). At 50¬įC, Kw > 10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ‚Āī ‚Üí pH < 7, but water is still neutral ([H‚Āļ] = [OH‚ĀĽ]).

points.title: Solubility product (Ksp)
points.content: For M‚āźNb ‚áĆ aM^(b+) + bN^(a-): Ksp = [M^(b+)]^a √ó [N^(a-)]^b. If ionic product > Ksp: precipitate forms. Solubility s relates to Ksp. Common ion effect: adding a common ion decreases solubility (drives equilibrium back).

points.title: Determining rate equation from initial rates
points.content: Compare experiments keeping all concentrations constant except one. If doubling [A] doubles rate ‚Üí 1st order in A. If doubling [A] quadruples rate ‚Üí 2nd order. If no change ‚Üí 0th order. Repeat for each reactant. Then calculate k from rate = k[A]^m[B]^n.

points.title: Graphical methods for order
points.content: 0th order: [A] vs t = straight line with negative gradient (gradient = ‚ąík). 1st order: ln[A] vs t = straight line (gradient = ‚ąík), constant t¬Ĺ. 2nd order: 1/[A] vs t = straight line (gradient = k).

points.title: Arrhenius equation and graph
points.content: k = A¬∑e^(‚ąíEa/RT). Taking ln: ln k = ln A ‚ąí Ea/RT. Plot ln k vs 1/T: gradient = ‚ąíEa/R ‚Üí Ea = ‚ąígradient √ó R. Intercept = ln A. As T increases, k increases (exponential relationship).

points.title: Deducing mechanism from rate equation
points.content: Rate equation indicates which species are involved in the rate-determining step (RDS) and their molecularity. Only species in the rate equation appear in the RDS. Example: rate = k[A][B¬≤] ‚Üí A and 2 molecules of B in RDS. Propose mechanism consistent with overall equation and rate law.

points.title: Catalysis and rate
points.content: Catalyst provides alternative pathway with lower Ea. Does NOT change őĒH or equilibrium position (for reversible reactions). Heterogeneous: different phase (Fe in Haber, Pt in catalytic converter). Homogeneous: same phase (H‚Āļ in esterification, Fe¬≤‚Āļ/Fe¬≥‚Āļ in S‚āāO‚āą¬≤‚ĀĽ + I‚ĀĽ reaction).

points.title: Entropy changes
points.content: S increases when: solid ‚Üí liquid ‚Üí gas; dissolving; mixing; number of gas moles increases; temperature increases. S decreases when: gases condense; precipitation; number of gas moles decreases. őĒSsystem = ő£S(products) ‚ąí ő£S(reactants).

points.title: Total entropy and spontaneity
points.content: őĒStotal = őĒSsystem + őĒSsurroundings. őĒSsurroundings = ‚ąíőĒH/T. A process is spontaneous when őĒStotal > 0. This is equivalent to őĒG < 0 (since őĒG = ‚ąíTőĒStotal).

points.title: őĒG analysis for different conditions
points.content: őĒH < 0, őĒS > 0: always spontaneous (őĒG always negative). őĒH > 0, őĒS < 0: never spontaneous. őĒH < 0, őĒS < 0: spontaneous at low T (when |TőĒS| < |őĒH|). őĒH > 0, őĒS > 0: spontaneous at high T (when TőĒS > őĒH). Feasibility temperature: T = őĒH/őĒS.

points.title: őĒG and equilibrium constant
points.content: őĒG¬į = ‚ąíRT ln K. If K > 1: őĒG¬į < 0 (products favoured). If K < 1: őĒG¬į > 0 (reactants favoured). If K = 1: őĒG¬į = 0. At equilibrium: őĒG = 0 (not őĒG¬į). Standard value vs actual value: őĒG = őĒG¬į + RT ln Q.

points.title: Electrochemistry link to őĒG
points.content: őĒG¬į = ‚ąínFE¬įcell. Where n = moles of electrons transferred, F = Faraday constant (96500 C mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ). If E¬įcell > 0: őĒG¬į < 0 (spontaneous). Consistent with K: őĒG¬į = ‚ąíRT ln K = ‚ąínFE¬į.

points.title: Definition and properties of transition elements
points.content: d-block elements that form at least one stable ion with incomplete d sub-shell (Ti to Cu; Sc and Zn excluded). Properties: variable oxidation states, coloured ions, catalytic activity, complex ion formation, paramagnetic.

points.title: Electronic configurations
points.content: General: [Ar] 3d^n 4s¬≤. Exceptions: Cr = [Ar] 3d‚ĀĶ 4s¬Ļ; Cu = [Ar] 3d¬Ļ‚Āį 4s¬Ļ (extra stability of half-full and full d shells). When forming ions: 4s electrons lost first, then 3d. Fe¬≥‚Āļ = [Ar] 3d‚ĀĶ (half-full d, stable).

points.title: Colour of complex ions
points.content: Due to d-d transition: ligands cause d orbital splitting (crystal field splitting). Electron absorbs visible light, moves from lower to higher d orbital. Complementary colour is observed. More splitting ‚Üí shorter wavelength absorbed ‚Üí different colour. [Cu(H‚āāO)‚āÜ]¬≤‚Āļ blue; [Cu(NH‚āÉ)‚āĄ]¬≤‚Āļ deep blue; [Fe(H‚āāO)‚āÜ]¬≥‚Āļ pale violet.

points.title: Common complex ions
points.content: [Fe(H‚āāO)‚āÜ]¬≤‚Āļ: green. [Fe(H‚āāO)‚āÜ]¬≥‚Āļ: pale violet/brown. [Cu(H‚āāO)‚āÜ]¬≤‚Āļ: light blue. [Cu(NH‚āÉ)‚āĄ(H‚āāO)‚āā]¬≤‚Āļ: deep blue. [Cr(H‚āāO)‚āÜ]¬≥‚Āļ: green-violet. [Co(H‚āāO)‚āÜ]¬≤‚Āļ: pink. [Fe(SCN)(H‚āāO)‚āÖ]¬≤‚Āļ: blood red (test for Fe¬≥‚Āļ). [FeCl‚āĄ]‚ĀĽ: yellow.

points.title: Ligand substitution reactions
points.content: H‚āāO replaced by stronger ligands. Cu¬≤‚Āļ(aq) + 4NH‚āÉ ‚Üí [Cu(NH‚āÉ)‚āĄ]¬≤‚Āļ + 4H‚āāO (deepen blue; add excess NH‚āÉ to Cu(OH)‚āā precipitate). Fe¬≥‚Āļ(aq) + SCN‚ĀĽ ‚Üí [Fe(SCN)]¬≤‚Āļ (blood red; test for Fe¬≥‚Āļ). Haemoglobin: Fe¬≤‚Āļ centre, O‚āā ligand; CO binds more strongly ‚Üí poisoning.

points.title: Redox reactions of vanadium
points.content: V can exist as +5, +4, +3, +2. Reduction by Zn in acidic solution: VO‚āā‚Āļ (yellow, +5) ‚Üí VO¬≤‚Āļ (blue, +4) ‚Üí V¬≥‚Āļ (green, +3) ‚Üí V¬≤‚Āļ (violet, +2). This demonstrates variable oxidation states of transition metals.

points.title: Stability constant (Kstab)
points.content: For [ML_n]^(a+) ‚áĆ M^(a+) + nL. Kstab = [M^(a+)][L]^n / [[ML_n]^(a+)] ... actually Kstab = product/reactants for formation. Large Kstab ‚Üí stable complex. EDTA‚Āī‚ĀĽ forms very stable complexes with 4-coordinate binding (chelation); used in medicine (treatment of metal poisoning).

points.title: Evidence for benzene structure
points.content: Kekul√©'s alternating double bonds predicts: two forms of 1,2-disubstituted benzene (not observed), high reactivity to Br‚āā (not seen), őĒHhyd more negative than expected. Actual evidence: all C-C bonds equal length (139 pm), thermodynamic stability (őĒHhyd less negative by ~150 kJ/mol = delocalisation energy), no addition reactions with bromine water.

points.title: Electrophilic substitution mechanism
points.content: General: Ar‚ÄďH + E‚Āļ ‚Üí [Ar‚ÄďH‚ÄďE]‚Āļ (arenium ion/ŌÉ-complex) ‚Üí Ar‚ÄďE + H‚Āļ. The ring is restored (aromaticity retained) by loss of H‚Āļ. Nitration: E‚Āļ = NO‚āā‚Āļ (from HNO‚āÉ + H‚āāSO‚āĄ). Halogenation: E‚Āļ from X‚āā + AlX‚āÉ (Lewis acid catalyst).

points.title: Nitration conditions and mechanism
points.content: Conc. HNO‚āÉ + conc. H‚āāSO‚āĄ, 50-55¬įC (not higher: polynitration). Generation of NO‚āā‚Āļ: HNO‚āÉ + H‚āāSO‚āĄ ‚Üí NO‚āā‚Āļ + HSO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ + H‚āāO. NO‚āā‚Āļ attacks ring ‚Üí ŌÉ-complex ‚Üí lose H‚Āļ to HSO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ ‚Üí nitrobenzene.

points.title: Friedel-Crafts reactions
points.content: Alkylation: ArH + RCl + AlCl‚āÉ ‚Üí Ar‚ÄďR + HCl + AlCl‚āÉ. Acylation: ArH + RCOCl + AlCl‚āÉ ‚Üí Ar‚ÄďCOR + HCl + AlCl‚āÉ. AlCl‚āÉ is Lewis acid catalyst: generates R‚Āļ or RCO‚Āļ (acylium). Acylation preferred in synthesis (no rearrangement, product deactivated ‚Üí monosubstitution only).

points.title: Directing effects of substituents
points.content: Activating (2,4-directors): ‚ÄďOH, ‚ÄďNH‚āā, ‚ÄďOCH‚āÉ (donate electrons into ring by resonance). Deactivating (3-directors): ‚ÄďNO‚āā, ‚ÄďCN, ‚ÄďCOOH, ‚ÄďCHO (withdraw electrons). Halogens: deactivating but 2,4-directors (lone pairs donate but electronegative). Use directing effects to plan synthesis routes.

points.title: Reactions of phenol
points.content: Phenol is more reactive than benzene (‚ÄďOH activates ring). Reacts with Br‚āā(aq) without catalyst ‚Üí 2,4,6-tribromophenol (white precipitate, test for phenol). Reacts with NaOH ‚Üí sodium phenoxide (phenol is weakly acidic, pKa ~10). Does NOT react with Na‚āāCO‚āÉ.

points.title: Reactivity order
points.content: Acyl chloride > acid anhydride > carboxylic acid > ester > amide. Decreasing reactivity towards nucleophiles (better leaving groups make more reactive). Acyl chlorides react violently with water; amides hydrolyse slowly.

points.title: Reactions of acyl chlorides
points.content: With water: RCOC1 + H‚āāO ‚Üí RCOOH + HCl (vigorous, fumes). With alcohol: RCOCl + R'OH ‚Üí RCOOR' + HCl (ester, no catalyst, room temperature). With amine: RCOCl + R'NH‚āā ‚Üí RCONHR' + HCl (amide). With ammonia: RCOCl + NH‚āÉ ‚Üí RCONH‚āā + HCl.

points.title: Reactions of acid anhydrides
points.content: Similar to acyl chlorides but less vigorous. With water: (RCO)‚āāO + H‚āāO ‚Üí 2RCOOH. With alcohol: ‚Üí ester + carboxylic acid (not ester + HCl). With amine: ‚Üí amide + carboxylic acid. Ethanoic anhydride used industrially (safer, no HCl evolved).

points.title: Mechanism: addition-elimination
points.content: Step 1: nucleophile (e.g. OH‚ĀĽ or H‚āāO) adds to C=O ‚Üí tetrahedral intermediate. Step 2: leaving group (Cl‚ĀĽ from acyl chloride, RCOO‚ĀĽ from anhydride) is expelled, C=O restored. Net result: substitution at carbonyl carbon.

points.title: Hydrolysis of esters
points.content: Acid hydrolysis (H‚āāSO‚āĄ/H‚āāO, heat): reversible, gives carboxylic acid + alcohol. Alkaline hydrolysis / saponification (NaOH/H‚āāO, heat): irreversible, gives carboxylate salt + alcohol (used in soap making). Alkaline faster and complete.

points.title: Basicity of amines
points.content: Amines are bases: lone pair on N accepts H‚Āļ. pKb: alkyl amines (~3-4, stronger than NH‚āÉ) < NH‚āÉ (~4.7) < aryl amines (~9). Phenylamine weaker base: lone pair delocalised into benzene ring. Alkyl groups donate electrons ‚Üí increase lone pair availability.

points.title: Reactions of amines
points.content: With acids: RNH‚āā + HCl ‚Üí RNH‚āÉ‚ĀļCl‚ĀĽ (salt). With acyl chlorides: ‚Üí amide + HCl. As nucleophile (nucleophilic substitution): attack halogenoalkane ‚Üí secondary amine (then tertiary, then quaternary ammonium). With nitrous acid: ArNH‚āā + HNO‚āā ‚Üí ArN‚āā‚Āļ (diazonium salt, 0-5¬įC).

points.title: Amino acids properties
points.content: Zwitterion: ‚ÄďNH‚āÉ‚Āļ and ‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ simultaneously. Isoelectric point (pI): pH at which molecule carries no net charge; amine and carboxyl groups both in ionic form. In acidic solution: ‚ÄďNH‚āÉ‚Āļ group predominates (cation). In alkaline: ‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ group (anion). Forms peptides via condensation.

points.title: Diazonium salt reactions
points.content: Formed: ArNH‚āā + NaNO‚āā + HCl, 0-5¬įC ‚Üí ArN‚āā‚ĀļCl‚ĀĽ. Must keep cold (decomposes above 5¬įC). Coupling with phenol (alkaline): ArN‚āā‚Āļ + ArOH ‚Üí Ar-N=N-Ar' (azo dye, orange-yellow). Coupling with aryl amines (weakly acidic): ‚Üí red/orange azo compound.

points.title: Polyamides (nylon)
points.content: Nylon-6,6: hexan-1,6-diamine + hexanedioic acid ‚Üí [-NH-(CH‚āā)‚āÜ-NH-CO-(CH‚āā)‚āĄ-CO-]n + H‚āāO. Amide bonds between each monomer unit. H-bonds between chains ‚Üí strong, tough fibre. Peptide bonds in proteins also amide bonds.

points.title: Addition polymers
points.content: Monomer contains C=C double bond. Opens to form long chain; no other product. Examples: poly(ethene) from ethene, PVC from chloroethene, PTFE from tetrafluoroethene. Repeat unit loses one C=C: ‚Äď[CH‚āā‚ÄďCHX]n‚Äď. Problems: non-biodegradable, difficult to recycle.

points.title: Condensation polymers
points.content: Two functional groups react, losing small molecule (H‚āāO or HCl) each time. Polyesters: diol + dicarboxylic acid ‚Üí ‚Äď[O‚ÄďR‚ÄďO‚ÄďCO‚ÄďR'‚ÄďCO]n‚Äď + H‚āāO. Polyamides: diamine + diacid (or diacyl chloride) ‚Üí ‚Äď[NH‚ÄďR‚ÄďNH‚ÄďCO‚ÄďR'‚ÄďCO]n‚Äď + H‚āāO (or HCl).

points.title: Drawing repeat units
points.content: Addition: draw the repeat unit from monomer (remove the double bond, put single bond between units). Condensation: identify the bonds formed between monomers (ester ‚ÄďCOO‚Äď or amide ‚ÄďCONH‚Äď); show the atoms lost. Include brackets with n.

points.title: Hydrolysis of condensation polymers
points.content: Polyesters hydrolyse in acid or alkali (break ester bonds ‚Üí diol + diacid/salt). Polyamides hydrolyse in acid or alkali (break amide bonds ‚Üí diamine + diacid). Addition polymers (C‚ÄďC backbone) do NOT hydrolyse. This is why polyesters/polyamides are biodegradable.

points.title: Kevlar and other engineering polymers
points.content: Kevlar: polyamide from benzene-1,4-diamine + benzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid. Highly ordered H-bonds between parallel chains ‚Üí exceptional strength. Used in body armour, tyres. Terylene (PET): polyester from ethylene glycol + terephthalic acid; used in fibres, bottles.

points.title: Key functional group interconversions
points.content: Alkene ‚Üí alkane (H‚āā/Ni), ‚Üí alcohol (H‚āāO/acid), ‚Üí halogenoalkane (HX), ‚Üí diol (cold KMnO‚āĄ). Halogenoalkane ‚Üí alcohol (NaOH/H‚āāO), ‚Üí nitrile (KCN/alc), ‚Üí amine (NH‚āÉ/alc), ‚Üí alkene (KOH/alc, hot). Primary alcohol ‚Üí aldehyde ‚Üí carboxylic acid. Secondary alcohol ‚Üí ketone. Aldehyde ‚Üí primary alcohol (NaBH‚āĄ).

points.title: Carbon chain extension methods
points.content: +1C: Nucleophilic substitution with KCN/HCN (nitrile) ‚Üí then hydrolysis to carboxylic acid. +1C: via Grignard reagent (A-level context: RMgX + CO‚āā ‚Üí carboxylic acid). Friedel-Crafts acylation extends chain on benzene ring.

points.title: Planning a synthesis route
points.content: 1. Identify carbon skeleton changes needed. 2. Identify functional group at start vs end. 3. Work backwards (retrosynthesis): what reaction produces target from simpler molecule? 4. Check reagents and conditions are known. 5. Consider stereochemistry (chiral products, geometric isomers).

points.title: Controlling reaction type
points.content: Halogenoalkane: with NaOH(aq) ‚Üí substitution (alcohol). With KOH in ethanol ‚Üí elimination (alkene). With NH‚āÉ(excess) ‚Üí amine. With KCN ‚Üí nitrile. Alcohol: oxidation conditions determine aldehyde vs carboxylic acid.

points.title: Protection of functional groups
points.content: When a reaction would attack a sensitive group, protect it first. Example: to reduce ester without reducing acid: convert acid to ester temporarily. In peptide synthesis: protect ‚ÄďNH‚āā as N-Boc amine, then deprotect. A-level: understand the principle of using protecting groups.

points.title: Mass spectrometry interpretation
points.content: Molecular ion (M‚Āļ): highest m/z peak (excluding M+1, M+2 isotope peaks) = Mr. Fragmentation: loss of groups gives fragment peaks. Common losses: ‚ąí15 (CH‚āÉ), ‚ąí29 (CHO or C‚āāH‚āÖ), ‚ąí45 (OEt), ‚ąí77 (C‚āÜH‚āÖ). Base peak: most abundant fragment ion.

points.title: IR spectroscopy key absorptions
points.content: O‚ÄďH (alcohol): broad 3230‚Äď3550 cm‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. O‚ÄďH (carboxylic acid): very broad 2500‚Äď3300 cm‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. N‚ÄďH: 3300‚Äď3500 cm‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. C‚ÄďH: 2850‚Äď3100 cm‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. C‚Č°N: 2200‚Äď2260 cm‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. C=O (carbonyl): strong, sharp 1680‚Äď1750 cm‚ĀĽ¬Ļ (ester ~1735, aldehyde/ketone ~1715, carboxylic acid ~1710). C‚ÄďO: 1050‚Äď1300 cm‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. Fingerprint region: 500‚Äď1500 cm‚ĀĽ¬Ļ.

points.title: ¬ĻH NMR chemical shifts
points.content: TMS = 0 ppm reference. R‚ÄďCH‚āÉ: ~0.9 ppm. R‚ÄďCH‚āā‚ÄďR: ~1.3 ppm. R‚ÄďCH=: 4.5‚Äď6.5 ppm (alkene). ArH: 6.5‚Äď8.5 ppm (benzene ring). R‚ÄďCHO: ~9‚Äď10 ppm (aldehyde). R‚ÄďCOOH: ~10‚Äď12 ppm (carboxylic acid). R‚ÄďOH: 1‚Äď5 ppm (variable). NH: 1‚Äď5 ppm. Electronegative groups shift nearby H downfield (+).

points.title: NMR spin-spin coupling
points.content: (n+1) rule: a proton with n equivalent neighbouring protons is split into (n+1) peaks. Doublet (d): 1 neighbour. Triplet (t): 2 neighbours. Quartet (q): 3 neighbours. Singlet (s): 0 neighbours. Integration gives relative number of each type of H. D‚āāO shake: exchangeable H (OH, NH) disappear.

points.title: Combining spectroscopic data
points.content: Strategy: 1. Find Mr from mass spectrum. 2. Calculate degrees of unsaturation: DoU = (2C + 2 + N ‚ąí H ‚ąí X)/2. 3. IR: identify functional groups (C=O, OH, NH). 4. ¬ĻH NMR: count types of H, integration, coupling pattern. 5. Piece together consistent structure.

points.title: Chromatography types
points.content: TLC (thin layer): stationary = silica, mobile = solvent. Rf = distance spot / distance solvent. GC: stationary = liquid on support, mobile = inert carrier gas; retention time identifies compound; used with MS for identification. HPLC: liquid mobile phase, high pressure; separates polar/ionic compounds. Paper chromatography: water in cellulose fibres is stationary phase.
